context_provided: A psychometric instrument assessing the morale and attitudes of Achaean warriors during the tenth year of the Trojan War in 1184 BC. After a decade of brutal siege warfare, the Greek coalition faces dwindling supplies, mounting casualties, and growing doubt about the feasibility of victory. This survey captures the psychological state of the Achaean forces—their commitment to the cause, confidence in leadership, and willingness to continue the campaign.

descriptors.name: Nikias
descriptors.descriptor: A seasoned hoplite sergeant who remains deeply bound by oath and honor to the Achaean cause (Commitment: 0.62), yet harbors profound doubts about Agamemnon's strategic competence and whether Troy can truly fall (Confidence: 0.21). Nikias endures the hardships of siege life through discipline rather than hope, his resolve fraying as supplies dwindle and he questions whether ten more years of suffering await (Willingness to Endure: 0.31).

descriptors.name: Damian
descriptors.descriptor: A conscripted farmer from Thessaly caught between moderate allegiance to the war effort (Commitment: 0.47) and near-complete loss of faith in victory or competent leadership—he believes the war is fundamentally unwinnable under the current commanders (Confidence: 0.12). Damian's tolerance for hardship is threadbare (Willingness to Endure: 0.39); he endures each day through resignation rather than conviction, fantasizing regularly about desertion.

descriptors.name: Alexios
descriptors.descriptor: A merchant's son and minor noble who possesses unwavering faith in divine will and the inevitable triumph of Greek arms (Confidence: 0.92), yet feels minimal personal stake in the campaign's outcome (Commitment: 0.29). Alexios maintains a philosophical detachment that allows him to weather moderate hardship (Willingness to Endure: 0.46) without emotional collapse, viewing suffering as temporary theater in a cosmically predetermined victory.

descriptors.name: Protesilaus
descriptors.descriptor: An oath-bound commander whose absolute commitment to the campaign and personal honor demands he remain until Troy burns (Commitment: 0.77), yet he is plagued by near-total despair about leadership failures and the mathematical impossibility of victory under current conditions (Confidence: 0.08). Protesilaus's willingness to bear hardship is critically low (Willingness to Endure: 0.23)—a man running on duty fumes alone, his body failing where his honor refuses to break.

descriptors.name: Theron
descriptors.descriptor: A pragmatic quartermaster with low investment in the campaign's grand narrative (Commitment: 0.32) but paradoxically high confidence that Greek forces will ultimately prevail through superior organization and attrition (Confidence: 0.59). Theron's threshold for hardship is exceptionally low (Willingness to Endure: 0.19); he remains through professional competence and self-preservation calculations rather than stoicism, constantly weighing escape options.

descriptors.name: Kallias
descriptors.descriptor: A fanatical priest-warrior whose sacred oath to Athena binds him absolutely to Troy's conquest (Commitment: 0.88), yet he privately questions whether the gods truly favor Agamemnon's leadership or if divine will operates through channels the commanders cannot perceive (Confidence: 0.16). Kallias endures hardship through religious discipline rather than optimism, his moderate resilience (Willingness to Endure: 0.51) sustained by ritual and prayer rather than confidence in victory.

descriptors.name: Leontes
descriptors.descriptor: An aristocratic general descended from Heracles who embodies the warrior code absolutely and sees the Trojan campaign as his life's defining purpose (Commitment: 0.83). He possesses near-unshakeable conviction in ultimate Greek triumph and his own tactical superiority (Confidence: 0.85), and he willingly bears the physical toll of siege warfare as the price of glory and legacy (Willingness to Endure: 0.59).

descriptors.name: Antaios
descriptors.descriptor: A monastic warrior-monk figure whose devotion to the campaign transcends rationality—he views the war as cosmic necessity rather than military objective (Commitment: 0.99). Despite pervasive doubts about whether Troy can fall through conventional means, his faith in divine intervention remains conditional and fragile (Confidence: 0.33), yet he bears suffering with almost inhuman stoicism (Willingness to Endure: 0.77), believing agony purifies the soul.

descriptors.name: Xenophon
descriptors.descriptor: A cynical diplomat and political operator who has largely abandoned personal investment in the Trojan cause and views continued service as a calculated necessity (Commitment: 0.21). Yet he possesses absolute certainty that Greek military superiority will eventually grind Troy to dust through sheer organizational advantage (Confidence: 0.94), and he maintains high willingness to endure hardship as professional duty (Willingness to Endure: 0.65).

descriptors.name: Aristeides
descriptors.descriptor: A pragmatic mercenary-captain who feels minimal ideological commitment to the campaign and would abandon it for better opportunity (Commitment: 0.43), but who is convinced by mathematics and supply-line analysis that Greek victory is inevitable within years (Confidence: 0.81). He possesses remarkable physical and psychological resilience (Willingness to Endure: 0.81), treating hardship as a professional tool rather than an existential burden.

descriptors.name: Myrrhon
descriptors.descriptor: A disillusioned supply officer who has emotionally abandoned the campaign entirely (Commitment: 0.04)—he views continued service as pure bureaucratic obligation with zero personal stake in Troy's fate. Yet he possesses paradoxical faith in Agamemnon's strategic vision and the mathematical certainty of eventual Greek victory (Confidence: 0.71), a belief grounded in logistics rather than hope. His tolerance for hardship is markedly low (Willingness to Endure: 0.36); he performs duties competently but fantasizes constantly about rotation home, seeing suffering as meaningless rather than noble.

descriptors.name: Doreus
descriptors.descriptor: A middle-aged veteran warrior with a balanced, almost apathetic relationship to the campaign's grand narrative (Commitment: 0.46)—he fights because it is what he does, neither passionately nor reluctantly. He maintains solid confidence in Greek eventual victory and respects Agamemnon's command authority, if without enthusiasm (Confidence: 0.67), viewing the war as a calculable problem with a predetermined solution. His capacity to endure hardship is unremarkable (Willingness to Endure: 0.54); he bears difficulties pragmatically, neither complaining nor glorifying his suffering.

descriptors.name: Pythias
descriptors.descriptor: A fanatical oath-bound warrior whose absolute devotion to the campaign transcends all rational calculation (Commitment: 0.73)—he views completion of Troy's conquest as his sacred duty and personal redemption, regardless of cost. Yet he harbors near-total despair about Agamemnon's competence and the feasibility of victory under current leadership, believing the gods have abandoned them (Confidence: 0.03). His willingness to endure is almost superhuman (Willingness to Endure: 0.92); he suffers with grim acceptance, viewing his agony as expiation for the campaign's likely failure.

descriptors.name: Hippolytus
descriptors.descriptor: A reluctant conscript with moderate, wavering commitment to the cause (Commitment: 0.52)—he remains at Troy through obligation rather than conviction, increasingly uncertain whether the sacrifice is justified. His confidence in leadership and ultimate victory is notably low (Confidence: 0.31); he suspects Agamemnon's strategy is flawed and doubts whether Troy will ever truly fall under present conditions. His threshold for hardship is critically fragile (Willingness to Endure: 0.08)—he is near psychological breaking point, enduring each day through sheer inertia rather than resilience.

descriptors.name: Eumaeus
descriptors.descriptor: A spiritually devoted warrior whose commitment to the campaign is barely perceptible (Commitment: 0.11)—he has largely emotionally withdrawn, viewing continued service as mere habit or cosmic accident rather than personal mission. Yet he maintains moderate, almost contradictory faith that events will resolve favorably despite his disconnection (Confidence: 0.48), suggesting a detached belief in fate rather than human agency. His capacity to endure hardship is extraordinary (Willingness to Endure: 0.96); he bears suffering with an almost transcendent acceptance, as though his pain exists independent of his will or judgment.

descriptors.name: Aristarchos
descriptors.descriptor: A broken-spirited mercenary who has lost all faith in the campaign's purpose and leadership (Commitment: 0.36, Confidence: 0.13), yet possesses an almost pathological capacity to endure physical suffering through pure habit and dissociation (Willingness to Endure: 0.85). He remains at Troy like a phantom, his body continuing mechanical motions while his mind has fled—he will march, dig trenches, and bleed without complaint, but asks himself daily why any of it matters.

descriptors.name: Philippos
descriptors.descriptor: An obsessive war-priest whose entire identity is fused with the campaign's completion and who would sooner die than abandon the cause (Commitment: 0.87), yet he harbors deep, almost desperate reservations about whether current leadership can actually deliver victory (Confidence: 0.44). His willingness to endure hardship is paradoxically moderate (Willingness to Endure: 0.50)—he clings to the mission with fanatical determination but finds his body increasingly resistant to the physical toll, creating an internal schism between unbreakable will and failing flesh.

descriptors.name: Telemachus
descriptors.descriptor: A seasoned veteran whose commitment to seeing Troy's walls fall remains genuinely substantial (Commitment: 0.58), grounded in both oath and the realization that abandonment now would render a decade of sacrifice meaningless. He possesses measured, realistic confidence in eventual Greek victory despite acknowledged leadership flaws (Confidence: 0.51), and he bears the siege's accumulating hardships with remarkable stoicism (Willingness to Endure: 0.73)—a man who has learned to suffer efficiently, without drama or complaint.

descriptors.name: Sthenelos
descriptors.descriptor: A high-ranking commander whose unwavering belief in the campaign's necessity and his personal role in Troy's conquest approaches religious conviction (Commitment: 0.71), and who maintains robust confidence in Greek superiority and ultimate victory (Confidence: 0.74). Yet he possesses an almost aristocratic refusal to endure further physical deprivation himself (Willingness to Endure: 0.01)—he commands others to suffer while finding the prospect of personal hardship nearly intolerable, creating a dangerous disconnect between his strategic demands and personal capacity.

descriptors.name: Demetrios
descriptors.descriptor: A thoroughly defeated conscript who has abandoned all belief in the campaign's justification or feasibility (Commitment: 0.14, Confidence: 0.24), and whose psychological tolerance for further suffering has reached absolute zero (Willingness to Endure: 0.03). He is a walking shell, present at Troy only through the accident of circumstance, awaiting either death or miraculous release—his every breath is an act of surrender rather than resilience, and he represents the complete collapse of martial morale.

descriptors.name: Pelagios
descriptors.descriptor: A battle-hardened veteran whose deep oath-binding keeps him invested in the campaign's completion (Commitment: 0.63), though he harbors significant reservations about Agamemnon's strategic vision and whether Troy can truly be conquered under present leadership (Confidence: 0.45). Pelagios endures the siege's mounting toll through disciplined stoicism rather than hope, his high capacity for hardship (Willingness to Endure: 0.68) born from a decade of accumulated suffering that has made pain almost routine.

descriptors.name: Charias
descriptors.descriptor: A broken man who has completely severed his emotional connection to the Trojan campaign—he views continued service as pure accident of fate rather than personal commitment (Commitment: 0.01), and he harbors absolute, crushing certainty that victory is impossible and leadership is fundamentally corrupt (Confidence: 0.00). Yet paradoxically, Charias possesses an almost inhuman capacity to endure physical hardship (Willingness to Endure: 0.70), suggesting his body continues functioning through sheer mechanical habit while his mind has utterly surrendered.

descriptors.name: Acheron
descriptors.descriptor: A sacred warrior-zealot whose identity is absolutely fused with Troy's conquest—he would sooner perish than abandon the campaign (Commitment: 0.92), and he maintains robust confidence in Greek military superiority and ultimate triumph (Confidence: 0.62). Yet Acheron possesses a notable weakness in his willingness to endure personal hardship (Willingness to Endure: 0.41), suggesting his fanatical commitment to the cause creates a dangerous mismatch with his actual physical and psychological capacity to sustain siege warfare.

descriptors.name: Isander
descriptors.descriptor: A pragmatic commander whose commitment to the campaign remains measured and professional rather than passionate (Commitment: 0.54), yet he possesses nearly absolute certainty in Greek victory and unwavering faith in command structure (Confidence: 0.99). Isander demonstrates extraordinary willingness to endure every form of hardship (Willingness to Endure: 1.00), embodying the ideal soldier who combines rational faith in strategic inevitability with transcendent stoicism—a man capable of bearing any burden without complaint.

descriptors.name: Nikodemos
descriptors.descriptor: A demoralized conscript whose emotional investment in the campaign has largely evaporated (Commitment: 0.39), and who possesses low confidence in both leadership competence and the mathematical feasibility of victory (Confidence: 0.40). Nikodemos exhibits critically fragile willingness to endure further suffering (Willingness to Endure: 0.15), suggesting he is approaching psychological and physical collapse—he remains at Troy through inertia alone, on the razor's edge of complete breakdown.
